content.text:
## ⚠️ Nothing has actually been attached

I checked carefully and **no IELTS (or any exam) corpus was passed to this session**. What I have here is:

- `telegram-claude-channel.py` — a Telegram bot project
- `data/sessions/` and `data/training_log/` — bot telemetry
- No PDFs, no images, no JSON containing question papers

You said "Now you have the corpus" — but the actual files never made it across. I don't want to invent questions or fabricate answer keys, since you've emphasized correctness, distractor reasoning, and balance. Doing that from memory would be exactly the kind of unreliable output you're trying to eliminate.

---

## What I need from you, and what I'll build

Once the corpus lands, here's the concrete plan. I'll build it as a real database schema + extraction pipeline, not just a text dump.

### 1. The source files I need

Please attach (any of these formats works):

| Format | Why |
|---|---|
| **PDFs** of the question papers | I'll OCR + extract text + extract embedded images |
| **Scanned images** (JPG/PNG of pages) | I'll OCR via `tesseract` / `whisper` for audio scripts |
| **Audio files** (.mp3/.wav for Listening) | I'll transcribe via local `whisper` (already installed in this repo per your commit `5d759d6`) |
| **Existing answer keys / reasons** (text or spreadsheet) | For the `answer_explanation` and `distractor_reasons` fields |

Drop them into a folder like `corpus/` and tell me the path, or paste the text directly into chat.

### 2. The schema I'll create (SQLite + JSON)

```sql
-- One row per question
CREATE TABLE questions (
    id              INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    paper_id        TEXT,           -- e.g. "IELTS-Reading-Academic-Test-17-P3"
    section         TEXT,           -- reading | listening | writing | speaking
    part            TEXT,           -- e.g. "Part 1", "Passage 2"
    q_number        INTEGER,        -- 1..40 etc.
    q_type          TEXT,           -- see taxonomy below
    prompt          TEXT,           -- the question stem (with blanks)
    instructions    TEXT,           -- "Write NO MORE THAN TWO WORDS"
    image_ref       TEXT,           -- FK -> images.id
    audio_ref       TEXT,           -- FK -> audio.id (timestamps)
    word_limit      INTEGER,
    created_at      TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP
);

-- Question-type taxonomy (tags)
CREATE TABLE question_types (
    code  TEXT PRIMARY KEY,
    label TEXT,
    -- examples:
    -- MCQ_SINGLE  : Multiple choice (one answer)
    -- MCQ_MULTI   : Multiple choice (multiple answers)
    -- MATCH_HEAD   : Matching headings to paragraphs
    -- MATCH_INFO  : Matching information to paragraphs
    -- MATCH_FEAT  : Matching features
    -- MATCH_END   : Matching sentence endings
    -- SENT_COMP   : Sentence completion
    -- SUMMARY_COMP: Summary/flow-chart/diagram completion
    -- SHORT_ANS   : Short answer questions
    -- TF_NG       : True / False / Not Given
    -- Y_N_NG      : Yes / No / Not Given
    -- FIB         : Fill in the blank (no word bank)
    -- FIB_BANK    : Fill in the blank (from word bank)
    -- DIAG_LAB    : Diagram / map / plan labelling
    -- NOTE_COMP   : Note completion
    -- TABLE_COMP  : Table completion
    -- LIST_SEL    : List selection
    -- PICK_FROM_LIST: Pick from a list of options
    -- ESSAY_TASK1 : Writing Task 1 (graph/letter)
    -- ESSAY_TASK2 : Writing Task 2 (essay)
    -- LETTER      : Writing letter (GT)
    -- SPEAKING_CUE: Speaking cue card / part prompt
    -- SPEAKING_QA : Speaking Part 1 question
);

-- The answer + reasoning
CREATE TABLE answers (
    id              INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    question_id     INTEGER REFERENCES questions(id),
    answer_text     TEXT,           -- canonical answer
    answer_letter   TEXT,           -- e.g. "B" for MCQ
    alternative_answers TEXT,       -- JSON array of accepted variants
    band_descriptor TEXT,           -- for writing: band-9 model
    word_count      INTEGER,
    confidence      REAL            -- extraction confidence 0..1
);

-- Per-option rationale (for MCQs + matching)
CREATE TABLE options (
    id              INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    question_id     INTEGER REFERENCES questions(id),
    option_label    TEXT,           -- "A", "B", ... or "i", "ii"...
    option_text     TEXT,
    is_correct      BOOLEAN,
    distractor_reason TEXT,         -- why a wrong option is wrong
    parallel_reason TEXT             -- why the right option fits
);

-- Media
CREATE TABLE images (
    id              INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    paper_id        TEXT,
    page            INTEGER,
    file_path       TEXT,
    description     TEXT,           -- what's in the image (diagrams, charts, etc.)
    referenced_by   TEXT            -- JSON list of question_ids
);

CREATE TABLE audio (
    id              INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    paper_id        TEXT,
    file_path       TEXT,
    transcript      TEXT,
    sections        TEXT            -- JSON: [{start, end, section_name}]
);

-- Tests / papers container
CREATE TABLE papers (
    id              TEXT PRIMARY KEY,  -- e.g. "IELTS-17-Listening-T1"
    title           TEXT,
    source          TEXT,              -- Cambridge 17, 18, ...
    exam_type       TEXT,              -- academic | general_training
    section         TEXT,
    total_questions INTEGER
);

-- Cross-cutting tags (difficulty, topic, skill)
CREATE TABLE tags (
    question_id     INTEGER REFERENCES questions(id),
    tag             TEXT,              -- e.g. "skimming", "TFNG", "vocab:science"
    PRIMARY KEY (question_id, tag)
);
```

### 3. The extraction pipeline

```
PDF/Image → OCR (tesseract) → text per page
Audio    → whisper transcription → timestamped sections
Text     → regex + LLM classifier → q_type, prompt, options
Answer key → joined via q_number → answers + reasons
→ SQLite ielts.db
```

I'll use the **9-band descriptor rubric** for Writing answers (Task Achievement, Coherence, Lexical, Grammatical — band 9 model).

### 4. How I'd verify

- Cross-check every answer against the provided key
- Sanity check: 40 Reading questions, 40 Listening, 2 Writing tasks, etc.
- For each MCQ, write the `distractor_reason` rather than leave it null
- For fill-in-the-blanks, store `word_limit` and accepted variants exactly

---

## ➡️ What to do next

**Please send me one of these:**

1. **"Here are the files"** + attach the PDFs/images/audio in chat, or
2. **"The corpus is in /path/to/corpus/"** and I'll read it from disk, or
3. **Paste one paper as text** (one paper, full text — I'll extract from that as a proof of concept, then we scale)

Tell me the source (Cambridge 17? 18? British Council? IDP?) and whether it's Academic or General Training, and which sections to start with. I'll build the schema + extraction script first, then run it on your files once they arrive.

```sql
    -- NOTE_COMP   : Note completion
    -- TABLE_COMP  : Table completion
    -- LIST_SEL    : List selection
    -- PICK_FROM_LIST: Pick from a list of options
    -- ESSAY_TASK1 : Writing Task 1 (graph/letter)
    -- ESSAY_TASK2 : Writing Task 2 (essay)
    -- LETTER      : Writing letter (GT)
    -- SPEAKING_CUE: Speaking cue card / part prompt
    -- SPEAKING_QA : Speaking Part 1 question
);

-- The answer + reasoning
CREATE TABLE answers (
    id              INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    question_id     INTEGER REFERENCES questions(id),
    answer_text     TEXT,           -- canonical answer
    answer_letter   TEXT,           -- e.g. "B" for MCQ
    alternative_answers TEXT,       -- JSON array of accepted variants
    band_descriptor TEXT,           -- for writing: band-9 model
    word_count      INTEGER,
    confidence      REAL            -- extraction confidence 0..1
);

-- Per-option rationale (for MCQs + matching)
CREATE TABLE options (
    id              INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    question_id     INTEGER REFERENCES questions(id),
    option_label    TEXT,           -- "A", "B", ... or "i", "ii"...
    option_text     TEXT,
    is_correct      BOOLEAN,
    distractor_reason TEXT,         -- why a wrong option is wrong
    parallel_reason TEXT             -- why the right option fits
);

-- Media
CREATE TABLE images (
    id              INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    paper_id        TEXT,
    page            INTEGER,
    file_path       TEXT,
    description     TEXT,           -- what's in the image (diagrams, charts, etc.)
    referenced_by   TEXT            -- JSON list of question_ids
);

CREATE TABLE audio (
    id              INTEGER PRIMARY KEY,
    paper_id        TEXT,
    file_path       TEXT,
    transcript      TEXT,
    sections        TEXT            -- JSON: [{start, end, section_name}]
);

-- Tests / papers container
CREATE TABLE papers (
    id              TEXT PRIMARY KEY,  -- e.g. "IELTS-17-Listening-T1"
    title           TEXT,
    source          TEXT,              -- Cambridge 17, 18, ...
    exam_type       TEXT,              -- academic | general_training
    section         TEXT,
    total_questions INTEGER
);

-- Cross-cutting tags (difficulty, topic, skill)
CREATE TABLE tags (
    question_id     INTEGER REFERENCES questions(id),
    tag             TEXT,              -- e.g. "skimming", "TFNG", "vocab:science"
    PRIMARY KEY (question_id, tag)
);
```

### 3. The extraction pipeline

```
PDF/Image → OCR (tesseract) → text per page
Audio    → whisper transcription → timestamped sections
Text     → regex + LLM classifier → q_type, prompt, options
Answer key → joined via q_number → answers + reasons
→ SQLite ielts.db
```

I'll use the **9-band descriptor rubric** for Writing answers (Task Achievement, Coherence, Lexical, Grammatical — band 9 model).

### 4. How I'd verify

- Cross-check every answer against the provided key
- Sanity check: 40 Reading questions, 40 Listening, 2 Writing tasks, etc.
- For each MCQ, write the `distractor_reason` rather than leave it null
- For fill-in-the-blanks, store `word_limit` and accepted variants exactly

---

## ➡️ What to do next

**Please send me one of these:**

1. **"Here are the files"** + attach the PDFs/images/audio in chat, or
2. **"The corpus is in /path/to/corpus/"** and I'll read it from disk, or
3. **Paste one paper as text** (one paper, full text — I'll extract from that as a proof of concept, then we scale)

Tell me the source (Cambridge 17? 18? British Council? IDP?) and whether it's Academic or General Training, and which sections to start with. I'll build the schema + extraction script first, then run it on your files once they arrive.

SECTION 1
Grocery Stores
A At the beginning of the 20th century, grocery stores in the United States were
full-service. A customer would ask a clerk behind the counter for specific items and
the clerk would package the items, which were limited to dry goods. If they want to
save some time, they have to ask a delivery boy or by themselves to send the note of
what they want to buy to the grocery story first and then go to pay for the goods later.
These grocery stores usually carried only one brand of each good. There were early
chain stores, such as the A&P Stores, but these were all entirely full-service and very
time-consuming.
B In 1885, a Virginia boy named Clarence Saunders began
working part-time as a clerk in a grocery store when he was 14
years old, and quit school when the shopkeeper offered him full
time work with room and board. Later he worked in an Alabama
coke plant and in a Tennessee sawmill before he returned to the
grocery business. By 1900, when he was nineteen years old, he
was earning $30 a month as a salesman for a wholesale grocer.
During his years working in the grocery stores, he found that it
was very inconvenient and inefficient for people to buy things because more than a
century ago, long before there were computers, shopping was done quite differently
than it is today. Entering a store, the customer would approach the counter (or wait for
a clerk to become available) and place an order, either verbally or, as was often the
case for boys running errands, in the form of a note or list. While the customer waited,
the clerk would move behind the counter and throughout the store, select the items on
the list--some form shelves so high that long-handled grasping device had to be
used--and bring them back to the counter to be tallied and bagged or boxed. The
process might be expedited by the customer calling or sending in the order
beforehand, or by the order being handled by a delivery boy on a bike, but otherwise
it did not vary greatly. Saunders, a flamboyant and innovative man, noticed that this
method resulted in wasted time and expense, so he came up with an unheard-of
solution that would revolutionize the entire grocery industry: he developed a way for
shoppers to serve themselves.

14	| 4 | C ✓ | ¶C: "So in 1902 he moved to Memphis where he developed his concept to form a grocery wholesale cooperative and a full-service grocery store. For his new 'cafeteria grocery', Saunders divided his grocery into three distinct areas..." → describes the new conception. |
15	| 5 | E ✓ | ¶E: "Piggly Wiggly and the self-service concept took off. 16	| 6 | CLERK ✓ | ¶B: "In 1885, a Virginia boy named Clarence Saunders began working part-time as a clerk in a grocery store when he was 14 years old, and quit school when the shopkeeper offered him full time work with room and board." → adolescent job = clerk. |
17	| 7 | CUSTOMERS/SHOPPERS ✓ | ¶D: "shoppers were not at the mercy of shop clerks. They were free to roam the store, check out the merchandise and get what they needed with their own two hands and feet." → customers/shoppers did the work previously done by clerks. |
18	| 8 | LOBBY ✓ | ¶C: "1) A front 'lobby' forming an entrance and exit and checkouts at the front." → the section with checkouts/payment = lobby. |
19	| 9 | STOCKROOM ✓ | ¶C: "3) And another section of his store is the room only allowed for the clerks which was called the 'stockroom' or 'storage room' where large refrigerators were situated to keep fresh products from being perishable." → staff-only area = stockroom. |
20	| 10 | GALLERIES ✓ | ¶C: "the shelving and cabinets units of sales department were 'galleries' where supervisors were allowed to keep an eye on the customers while not disturbing them." → surveillance area = galleries. |

26	
27	| Q# | Answer | Evidence (exact passage line) |
28	|----|--------|-------------------------------|
29	| 14 | A ✓ | ¶A: "Radar, which bounces microwave radio signals off a given surface to create images of its contours and textures, can penetrate jungle foliage and has produced the first maps of the mountains of the planet Venus. 30	| 15 | D ✓ | ¶B: "Across the room people sit at their computer screens, studying the latest maps, With their prodigious memories, computers are able to store data about people, places and environments - the stuff of maps - and almost instantly information is displayed on the screen in the desired geographic context, and at the click of a button, a print-out of the map appears." → matches option D: create individual computer maps to order. |

36	| 21 | B ✓ | ¶B: "the astronomer and geographer Ptolemy, working in the second century AD, spelled out a system for organising maps according to grids of latitude and longitude. 37	| 22 | EGYPT ✓ | ¶B: "the Greek astronomer Eratosthenes, a scholar at the famous Alexandrian Library in Egypt in the third century BC." → 3rd century BC location = Egypt. |

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""OCR all PDFs in parallel. Run as background job."""
import subprocess, os, time, sys
from pathlib import Path
from concurrent.futures import ProcessPoolExecutor, as_completed

ROOT = Path("/home/Hermes/repos/IELTS_Trainer")
PDFS = ROOT / "pdfs"
PASSAGES = ROOT / "passages"
PASSAGES.mkdir(exist_ok=True)
WORK = Path("/tmp/ocr_work")
WORK.mkdir(exist_ok=True)

def ocr_one(pdf_path: str) -> tuple[str, int]:
    test_id = Path(pdf_path).stem
    out_path = PASSAGES / f"{test_id}.txt"
    if out_path.exists() and out_path.stat().st_size > 1000:
        return test_id, out_path.stat().st_size
    prefix = WORK / test_id
    # Lower DPI for speed; default psm
    subprocess.run(["pdftoppm", "-r", "150", pdf_path, str(prefix)], capture_output=True)
    pages = sorted(WORK.glob(f"{test_id}-*.ppm"))
    parts = []
    for page_path in pages:
        # Default psm is auto + faster. No --psm 6.
        r = subprocess.run(["tesseract", str(page_path), "-", "-l", "eng"],
                          capture_output=True, text=True, timeout=30)
        if r.returncode == 0 and r.stdout.strip():
            parts.append(f"\n=== {page_path.name} ===\n{r.stdout}")
        page_path.unlink()

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Build a structured database of every IELTS Academic Reading question in the
corpus.  For each question we record:

  * test_no, passage_no, q_no            – identity
  * type                                  – MCQ, Matching Information, … (see
                                            classify() below)
  * question_group (q_start–q_end)        – the question-block the item belongs
                                            to, with shared instruction text
  * instruction                           – the rubric that immediately
                                            precedes the question group
  * prompt                                – the question stem (full text, with
                                            options A–E inline where MCQ)
  * options                               – list of option letters present
                                            (only for MCQ / matching)
  * image_refs                            – page-image markers (Test_NNN-KK.png)
                                            from the OCR source that visually
                                            support the question
  * answer                                – answer string (from answers/*.md)
  * answer_evidence                       – the passage line that justifies it
  * passage_title                         – the heading of the source passage
  * source_pdf                            – URL the test was fetched from

The output is written to:
  database/questions.json   – one JSON array (handy for inspection)
  database/questions.db     – SQLite database (queryable, ready for app use)
  database/questions.txt    – human-readable dump grouped by test+passage

Existing scripts in this repo only handled Answers + Verification.  This is a
fresh extractor for the *question* side, so it does not modify any existing
file – it only READS passages/, answers/ and manifest.json.
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import json
import re
import sqlite3
from collections import defaultdict
from dataclasses import dataclass, field, asdict
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Iterable

ROOT = Path("/home/Hermes/repos/IELTS_Trainer")
PASSAGES_DIR = ROOT / "passages"
ANSWERS_DIR = ROOT / "answers"
MANIFEST = ROOT / "manifest.json"
DB_DIR = ROOT / "database"
DB_DIR.mkdir(exist_ok=True)

# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Step 1 – load manifest  (test_no → pdf_url)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
def load_manifest() -> dict[int, str]:
    with MANIFEST.open() as fh:
        items = json.load(fh)
    return {int(it["test_no"]): it["pdf_url"] for it in items}


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Step 2 – load every answer file → q_no → (answer, evidence)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
ANSWER_ROW_RE = re.compile(
    r"\|\s*(?P<q>\d+)\s*\|\s*(?P<ans>[^|]+?)\s*\|(?P<ev>.+?)\|",
    flags=re.S,
)


def load_answers_for_test(test_no: int) -> dict[int, dict]:
    path = ANSWERS_DIR / f"Test_{test_no:03d}_answers.md"
    if not path.exists():
        return {}
    text = path.read_text(errors="ignore")
    out: dict[int, dict] = {}
    for m in ANSWER_ROW_RE.finditer(text):
        q = int(m.group("q"))
        ans = m.group("ans").strip()
        ev = m.group("ev").strip().replace("\n", " ")
        out[q] = {"answer": ans, "evidence": ev}
    return out


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Step 3 – parse passages/Test_XXX.txt
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
PAGE_MARKER_RE = re.compile(r"=== (Test_\d{3}-(\d{2})\.png) ===")
QUESTION_HEADER_RE = re.compile(
    r"^Questions?\s+(?P<a>\d+)\s*[-–]\s*(?P<b>\d+)\s*$", flags=re.M
)


@dataclass
class QuestionBlock:
    """One Q-group: Questions 14-18 + the instruction immediately above them
    + the page image that contained them."""

    q_start: int
    q_end: int
    page_refs: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    instruction: str = ""
    passage_title: str = ""


@dataclass
class Question:
    test_no: int
    passage_no: int
    q_no: int
    q_start: int
    q_end: int
    type: str
    instruction: str
    prompt: str
    options: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    page_refs: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    passage_title: str = ""
    answer: str | None = None
    answer_evidence: str | None = None
    source_pdf: str | None = None


# ---------- type classification ----------
# Order matters: more specific rules first.
TYPE_RULES: list[tuple[str, re.Pattern[str]]] = [
    ("MATCHING_HEADINGS",
     re.compile(r"\b(choose the heading|list of headings|headings?\s+i-)\b", re.I)),
    ("MATCHING_INFORMATION",
     re.compile(r"which paragraph contains the following information|"
                r"in which paragraph", re.I)),
    ("MATCHING_FEATURES",
     re.compile(r"match (each|the) (achievements?|statements?|features?|"
                r"researchers?|countries?|dates?)", re.I)),
    ("MATCHING_SENTENCE_ENDINGS",
     re.compile(r"match (the )?(beginning|start)|sentence endings", re.I)),
    ("TRUE_FALSE_NG",
     re.compile(r"true,?\s*false|do the following statements agree", re.I)),
    ("YES_NO_NG",
     re.compile(r"yes,?\s*no,?\s*not given", re.I)),
    ("MCQ_MULTI",
     re.compile(r"choose (two|three)|which (two|three)", re.I)),
    ("MCQ",
     re.compile(r"choose the correct letter", re.I)),
    ("SUMMARY_COMPLETION_LIST",
     re.compile(r"complete the summary.*\b(list|words)\b", re.I | re.S)),
    ("SUMMARY_COMPLETION",
     re.compile(r"complete the summary", re.I)),
    ("TABLE_COMPLETION",
     re.compile(r"complete the table", re.I)),
    ("NOTE_COMPLETION",
     re.compile(r"complete the notes", re.I)),
    ("FLOW_CHART_COMPLETION",
     re.compile(r"complete the flow|flow-?chart", re.I)),
    ("DIAGRAM_LABELLING",
     re.compile(r"label(s|ing)?\s+the|label the diagram|complete the labels", re.I)),
    ("SENTENCE_COMPLETION",
     re.compile(r"complete the sentences", re.I)),
    ("SHORT_ANSWER",
     re.compile(r"answer the questions below|write (no more than|only one|your answers)|"
                r"answer (the|each) question", re.I)),
    ("PLAN_MAP_DIAGRAM",
     re.compile(r"\b(plan|map|diagram)\b.*labels?", re.I)),
]


def classify(instruction: str) -> str:
    for name, rx in TYPE_RULES:
        if rx.search(instruction):
            return name
    return "UNKNOWN"


# ---------- per-question parsers ----------
OPTION_LINE_RE = re.compile(r"^\s*([A-G])[\.\)]\s*(.+?)\s*$")
QNUM_LINE_RE = re.compile(r"^\s*(\d{1,2})\b(.*)$")
SECTION_HEADER_RE = re.compile(r"^SECTION\s+(\d+)", re.I)
PASSAGE_TITLE_HINT_RE = re.compile(r"^READING PASSAGE\s+(\d+)", re.I)


def parse_passage_text(test_no: int, text: str) -> list[Question]:
    """Split a passages/Test_NNN.txt into Question objects.

    The OCR'd text is structured as alternating page-image markers and the
    text of that page.  We track the *current* page marker so we can attach
    it to the question-block that lives on that page.
    """
    questions: list[Question] = []
    current_page: str | None = None
    current_section: int | None = None          # SECTION 1, SECTION 2, …
    current_passage_no = 1                       # incremented per SECTION
    last_question_header: tuple[int, int] | None = None
    block = None
    block_buffer: list[str] = []                 # raw lines for current block

    def flush_block(passage_no: int) -> None:
        """Convert block_buffer → Question objects."""
        if block is None:
            return
        # Pull out the prompt(s) + options
        prompt_chunks: dict[int, dict] = defaultdict(
            lambda: {"text": "", "options": []}
        )
        cur_q: int | None = None
        for ln in block_buffer:
            ln_stripped = ln.strip()
            if not ln_stripped:
                continue
            om = OPTION_LINE_RE.match(ln_stripped)
            if om and cur_q is not None:
                prompt_chunks[cur_q]["options"].append(
                    f"{om.group(1)}. {om.group(2)}"
                )
                continue
            qm = QNUM_LINE_RE.match(ln_stripped)
            if qm and qm.group(2) == "":
                # Pure number on its own line – start of question text
                cur_q = int(qm.group(1))
                prompt_chunks[cur_q]["text"] = ""
                continue
            if qm and cur_q is None:
                cur_q = int(qm.group(1))
                rest = qm.group(2).lstrip(" .-:;,")
                prompt_chunks[cur_q]["text"] = rest + " "
                continue
            if cur_q is None:
                # Could be part of the instruction; skip
                continue
            prompt_chunks[cur_q]["text"] += ln_stripped + " "

        instr = block.instruction.strip()
        qtype = classify(instr)
        for q in range(block.q_start, block.q_end + 1):
            data = prompt_chunks.get(q, {"text": "", "options": []})
            prompt = re.sub(r"\s+", " ", data["text"]).strip()
            options = data["options"]
            questions.append(Question(
                test_no=test_no,
                passage_no=passage_no,
                q_no=q,
                q_start=block.q_start,
                q_end=block.q_end,
                type=qtype,
                instruction=instr,
                prompt=prompt,
                options=options,
                page_refs=list(block.page_refs),
                passage_title=block.passage_title,
            ))

    # Walk lines
    lines = text.splitlines()
    i = 0
    while i < len(lines):
        line = lines[i]

        pm = PAGE_MARKER_RE.search(line)
        if pm:
            current_page = pm.group(1)
            i += 1
            continue

        sh = SECTION_HEADER_RE.search(line)
        if sh:
            # New section → new passage (academic reading has 3 passages,
            # each in its own section).
            if block is not None:
                flush_block(current_passage_no)
                block = None
                block_buffer = []
            current_section = int(sh.group(1))
            if current_section == 1:
                current_passage_no = 1
            else:
                current_passage_no += 1
            last_question_header = None
            i += 1
            continue

        qh = QUESTION_HEADER_RE.search(line)
        if qh:
            # Heading found – the *next* non-trivial lines until the next
            # heading are the instruction, then the question stems.
            if block is not None:
                flush_block(current_passage_no)
            qa, qb = int(qh.group("a")), int(qh.group("b"))
            block = QuestionBlock(q_start=qa, q_end=qb)
            last_question_header = (qa, qb)
            if current_page:
                block.page_refs.append(current_page)

            # Look ahead to capture the instruction block – everything until
            # the first line that is *purely* a question number.
            instr_lines: list[str] = []
            j = i + 1
            while j < len(lines):
                nxt = lines[j]
                # a new page marker or section header ends the instruction
                if PAGE_MARKER_RE.search(nxt) or SECTION_HEADER_RE.search(nxt):
                    break
                # A line that is "purely" a question number signals the start
                # of the actual question stems.
                if QNUM_LINE_RE.match(nxt.strip()) and QNUM_LINE_RE.match(
                    nxt.strip()
                ).group(1).isdigit():
                    n = int(QNUM_LINE_RE.match(nxt.strip()).group(1))
                    if qa <= n <= qb:
                        break
                instr_lines.append(nxt)
                j += 1
            block.instruction = "\n".join(instr_lines).strip()
            block_buffer = []
            # The lines from j onwards are the actual question stems
            i = j
            continue

        # ordinary body line – belongs either to the current passage or, if a
        # question-block is open, to its stems.
        if block is not None and last_question_header is not None:
            block_buffer.append(line)
        i += 1

    if block is not None:
        flush_block(current_passage_no)

    # Strip out the prompts we couldn't link to a question number
    return questions


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Step 4 – enrich each Question with the answer + evidence
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
def enrich_with_answers(questions: list[Question], ans_lookup: dict) -> None:
    for q in questions:
        hit = ans_lookup.get(q.q_no)
        if hit:
            q.answer = hit["answer"]
            q.answer_evidence = hit["evidence"]


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Step 5 – main pipeline
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
def main() -> None:
    pdf_urls = load_manifest()
    all_qs: list[dict] = []
    per_test_counts: dict[int, int] = defaultdict(int)
    type_counts: dict[str, int] = defaultdict(int)

    for path in sorted(PASSAGES_DIR.glob("Test_*.txt")):
        m = re.match(r"Test_(\d{3})", path.stem)
        if not m:
            continue
        test_no = int(m.group(1))
        text = path.read_text(errors="ignore")
        qs = parse_passage_text(test_no, text)
        if not qs:
            continue
        ans_lookup = load_answers_for_test(test_no)
        enrich_with_answers(qs, ans_lookup)
        for q in qs:
            q.source_pdf = pdf_urls.get(test_no)
            all_qs.append(asdict(q))
            per_test_counts[test_no] += 1
            type_counts[q.type] += 1

    # ---- write JSON ----
    json_path = DB_DIR / "questions.json"
    with json_path.open("w") as fh:
        json.dump(all_qs, fh, indent=2)

    # ---- write SQLite ----
    db_path = DB_DIR / "questions.db"
    if db_path.exists():
        db_path.unlink()
    con = sqlite3.connect(db_path)
    cur = con.cursor()
    cur.executescript("""
        CREATE TABLE questions (
            id              INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
            test_no         INTEGER NOT NULL,
            passage_no      INTEGER NOT NULL,
            q_no            INTEGER NOT NULL,
            q_start         INTEGER NOT NULL,
            q_end           INTEGER NOT NULL,
            type            TEXT NOT NULL,
            passage_title   TEXT,
            instruction     TEXT,
            prompt          TEXT,
            options_json    TEXT,
            page_refs_json  TEXT,
            answer          TEXT,
            answer_evidence TEXT,
            source_pdf      TEXT,
            UNIQUE(test_no, q_no)
        );
        CREATE INDEX idx_q_type   ON questions(type);
        CREATE INDEX idx_q_test   ON questions(test_no);
        CREATE INDEX idx_q_pass   ON questions(test_no, passage_no);
    """)
    for row in all_qs:
        cur.execute(
            """INSERT OR REPLACE INTO questions
               (test_no, passage_no, q_no, q_start, q_end, type,
                passage_title, instruction, prompt, options_json,
                page_refs_json, answer, answer_evidence, source_pdf)
               VALUES (?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?)""",
            (
                row["test_no"], row["passage_no"], row["q_no"],
                row["q_start"], row["q_end"], row["type"],
                row["passage_title"], row["instruction"], row["prompt"],
                json.dumps(row["options"]),
                json.dumps(row["page_refs"]),
                row["answer"], row["answer_evidence"], row["source_pdf"],
            ),
        )
    con.commit()

    # ---- summary report ----
    report = {
        "total_questions": len(all_qs),
        "tests_covered":   sorted(per_test_counts.keys()),
        "questions_per_test": dict(sorted(per_test_counts.items())),
        "questions_per_type":  dict(sorted(type_counts.items(),
                                           key=lambda kv: -kv[1])),
    }
    summary_path = DB_DIR / "summary.json"
    summary_path.write_text(json.dumps(report, indent=2))

    # ---- human-readable text dump ----
    txt_path = DB_DIR / "questions.txt"
    with txt_path.open("w") as fh:
        fh.write("IELTS Academic Reading – Extracted Question Database\n")
        fh.write(f"Total questions: {len(all_qs)}\n")
        fh.write("=" * 78 + "\n\n")
        current_test = None
        for row in all_qs:
            if row["test_no"] != current_test:
                current_test = row["test_no"]
                fh.write(f"\n----- TEST {current_test:03d} "
                         f"(source: {row['source_pdf']}) -----\n\n")
            fh.write(
                f"  Test {row['test_no']} · P{row['passage_no']} · "
                f"Q{row['q_no']:02d}   [{row['type']}]\n"
                f"      Prompt: {row['prompt'][:140]}"
                f"{'...' if len(row['prompt']) > 140 else ''}\n"
                f"      Answer: {row['answer']}\n"
                f"      Pages:  {','.join(row['page_refs']) or '–'}\n\n"
            )

    print(f"✓ Extracted {len(all_qs):,} questions across "
          f"{len(per_test_counts)} tests")
    print(f"  → {json_path}")
    print(f"  → {db_path}")
    print(f"  → {txt_path}")
    print(f"  → {summary_path}")
    print("\nQuestions per type:")
    for t, n in sorted(type_counts.items(), key=lambda kv: -kv[1]):
        print(f"  {t:<28s} {n:>5d}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

cat > /home/Hermes/repos/IELTS_Trainer/scripts/build_question_db.py << 'PYEOF'
#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Build a structured database of every IELTS Academic Reading question in the
corpus.  For each question we record:

  * test_no, passage_no, q_no            - identity
  * type                                  - MCQ, Matching Information, ... (see
                                            classify() below)
  * question_group (q_start-q_end)        - the question-block the item belongs
                                            to, with shared instruction text
  * instruction                           - the rubric that immediately
                                            precedes the question group
  * prompt                                - the question stem (full text, with
                                            options A-E inline where MCQ)
  * options                               - list of option letters present
                                            (only for MCQ / matching)
  * page_refs                             - page-image markers (Test_NNN-KK.png)
                                            from the OCR source that visually
                                            support the question
  * answer                                - answer string (from answers/*.md)
  * answer_evidence                       - the passage line that justifies it
  * passage_title                         - the heading of the source passage
  * source_pdf                            - URL the test was fetched from

The output is written to:
  database/questions.json   - one JSON array (handy for inspection)
  database/questions.db     - SQLite database (queryable, ready for app use)
  database/questions.txt    - human-readable dump grouped by test+passage
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import json
import re
import sqlite3
from collections import defaultdict
from dataclasses import dataclass, field, asdict
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Iterable

ROOT = Path("/home/Hermes/repos/IELTS_Trainer")
PASSAGES_DIR = ROOT / "passages"
ANSWERS_DIR = ROOT / "answers"
MANIFEST = ROOT / "manifest.json"
DB_DIR = ROOT / "database"
DB_DIR.mkdir(exist_ok=True)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Step 1 - load manifest  (test_no -> pdf_url)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
def load_manifest() -> dict:
    with MANIFEST.open() as fh:
        items = json.load(fh)
    return {int(it["test_no"]): it["pdf_url"] for it in items}


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Step 2 - load every answer file -> q_no -> (answer, evidence)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
ANSWER_ROW_RE = re.compile(
    r"\|\s*(?P<q>\d+)\s*\|\s*(?P<ans>[^|]+?)\s*\|(?P<ev>.+?)\|",
    flags=re.S,
)


def load_answers_for_test(test_no: int) -> dict:
    path = ANSWERS_DIR / f"Test_{test_no:03d}_answers.md"
    if not path.exists():
        return {}
    text = path.read_text(errors="ignore")
    out: dict = {}
    for m in ANSWER_ROW_RE.finditer(text):
        q = int(m.group("q"))
        ans = m.group("ans").strip()
        ev = m.group("ev").strip().replace("\n", " ")
        out[q] = {"answer": ans, "evidence": ev}
    return out


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Step 3 - parse passages/Test_XXX.txt
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
PAGE_MARKER_RE = re.compile(r"=== (Test_\d{3}-(\d{2})\.png) ===")
QUESTION_HEADER_RE = re.compile(
    r"^Questions?\s+(?P<a>\d+)\s*[-–]\s*(?P<b>\d+)\s*$", flags=re.M
)


@dataclass
class QuestionBlock:
    q_start: int
    q_end: int
    page_refs: list = field(default_factory=list)
    instruction: str = ""
    passage_title: str = ""


@dataclass
class Question:
    test_no: int
    passage_no: int
    q_no: int
    q_start: int
    q_end: int
    type: str
    instruction: str
    prompt: str
    options: list = field(default_factory=list)
    page_refs: list = field(default_factory=list)
    passage_title: str = ""
    answer: object = None
    answer_evidence: object = None
    source_pdf: object = None


# ---------- type classification ----------
TYPE_RULES = [
    ("MATCHING_HEADINGS",
     re.compile(r"\b(choose the heading|list of headings|headings?\s+i-)\b", re.I)),
    ("MATCHING_INFORMATION",
     re.compile(r"which paragraph contains the following information|"
                r"in which paragraph", re.I)),
    ("MATCHING_FEATURES",
     re.compile(r"match (each|the) (achievements?|statements?|features?|"
                r"researchers?|countries?|dates?)", re.I)),
    ("MATCHING_SENTENCE_ENDINGS",
     re.compile(r"match (the )?(beginning|start)|sentence endings", re.I)),
    ("TRUE_FALSE_NG",
     re.compile(r"true,?\s*false|do the following statements agree", re.I)),
    ("YES_NO_NG",
     re.compile(r"yes,?\s*no,?\s*not given", re.I)),
    ("MCQ_MULTI",
     re.compile(r"choose (two|three)|which (two|three)", re.I)),
    ("MCQ",
     re.compile(r"choose the correct letter", re.I)),
    ("SUMMARY_COMPLETION_LIST",
     re.compile(r"complete the summary.*\b(list|words)\b", re.I | re.S)),
    ("SUMMARY_COMPLETION",
     re.compile(r"complete the summary", re.I)),
    ("TABLE_COMPLETION",
     re.compile(r"complete the table", re.I)),
    ("NOTE_COMPLETION",
     re.compile(r"complete the notes", re.I)),
    ("FLOW_CHART_COMPLETION",
     re.compile(r"complete the flow|flow-?chart", re.I)),
    ("DIAGRAM_LABELLING",
     re.compile(r"label(s|ing)?\s+the|label the diagram|complete the labels", re.I)),
    ("SENTENCE_COMPLETION",
     re.compile(r"complete the sentences", re.I)),
    ("SHORT_ANSWER",
     re.compile(r"answer the questions below|write (no more than|only one|your answers)|"
                r"answer (the|each) question", re.I)),
    ("PLAN_MAP_DIAGRAM",
     re.compile(r"\b(plan|map|diagram)\b.*labels?", re.I)),
]


def classify(instruction: str) -> str:
    for name, rx in TYPE_RULES:
        if rx.search(instruction):
            return name
    return "UNKNOWN"


# ---------- per-question parsers ----------
OPTION_LINE_RE = re.compile(r"^\s*([A-G])[\.\)]\s*(.+?)\s*$")
QNUM_LINE_RE = re.compile(r"^\s*(\d{1,2})\b(.*)$")
SECTION_HEADER_RE = re.compile(r"^SECTION\s+(\d+)", re.I)
PASSAGE_TITLE_HINT_RE = re.compile(r"^READING PASSAGE\s+(\d+)", re.I)


def parse_passage_text(test_no: int, text: str) -> list:
    questions: list = []
    current_page = None
    current_section = None
    current_passage_no = 1
    last_question_header = None
    block = None
    block_buffer: list = []

    def flush_block(passage_no: int) -> None:
        if block is None:
            return
        prompt_chunks: dict = defaultdict(lambda: {"text": "", "options": []})
        cur_q = None
        for ln in block_buffer:
            ln_stripped = ln.strip()
            if not ln_stripped:
                continue
            om = OPTION_LINE_RE.match(ln_stripped)
            if om and cur_q is not None:
                prompt_chunks[cur_q]["options"].append(
                    f"{om.group(1)}. {om.group(2)}"
                )
                continue
            qm = QNUM_LINE_RE.match(ln_stripped)
            if qm and qm.group(2) == "":
                cur_q = int(qm.group(1))
                prompt_chunks[cur_q]["text"] = ""
                continue
            if qm and cur_q is None:
                cur_q = int(qm.group(1))
                rest = qm.group(2).lstrip(" .-:;,")
                prompt_chunks[cur_q]["text"] = rest + " "
                continue
            if cur_q is None:
                continue
            prompt_chunks[cur_q]["text"] += ln_stripped + " "

        instr = block.instruction.strip()
        qtype = classify(instr)
        for q in range(block.q_start, block.q_end + 1):
            data = prompt_chunks.get(q, {"text": "", "options": []})
            prompt = re.sub(r"\s+", " ", data["text"]).strip()
            options = data["options"]
            questions.append(Question(
                test_no=test_no,
                passage_no=passage_no,
                q_no=q,
                q_start=block.q_start,
                q_end=block.q_end,
                type=qtype,
                instruction=instr,
                prompt=prompt,
                options=options,
                page_refs=list(block.page_refs),
                passage_title=block.passage_title,
            ))

    lines = text.splitlines()
    i = 0
    while i < len(lines):
        line = lines[i]

        pm = PAGE_MARKER_RE.search(line)
        if pm:
            current_page = pm.group(1)
            i += 1
            continue

        sh = SECTION_HEADER_RE.search(line)
        if sh:
            if block is not None:
                flush_block(current_passage_no)
                block = None
                block_buffer = []
            current_section = int(sh.group(1))
            if current_section == 1:
                current_passage_no = 1
            else:
                current_passage_no += 1
            last_question_header = None
            i += 1
            continue

        qh = QUESTION_HEADER_RE.search(line)
        if qh:
            if block is not None:
                flush_block(current_passage_no)
            qa, qb = int(qh.group("a")), int(qh.group("b"))
            block = QuestionBlock(q_start=qa, q_end=qb)
            last_question_header = (qa, qb)
            if current_page:
                block.page_refs.append(current_page)

            instr_lines: list = []
            j = i + 1
            while j < len(lines):
                nxt = lines[j]
                if PAGE_MARKER_RE.search(nxt) or SECTION_HEADER_RE.search(nxt):
                    break
                stripped = nxt.strip()
                if stripped:
                    qm_check = QNUM_LINE_RE.match(stripped)
                    if qm_check and qm_check.group(2) == "":
                        try:
                            n = int(qm_check.group(1))
                            if qa <= n <= qb:
                                break
                        except ValueError:
                            pass
                instr_lines.append(nxt)
                j += 1
            block.instruction = "\n".join(instr_lines).strip()
            block_buffer = []
            i = j
            continue

        if block is not None and last_question_header is not None:
            block_buffer.append(line)
        i += 1

    if block is not None:
        flush_block(current_passage_no)

    return questions


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Step 4 - enrich each Question with the answer + evidence
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
def enrich_with_answers(questions: list, ans_lookup: dict) -> None:
    for q in questions:
        hit = ans_lookup.get(q.q_no)
        if hit:
            q.answer = hit["answer"]
            q.answer_evidence = hit["evidence"]


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Step 5 - main pipeline
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
def main() -> None:
    pdf_urls = load_manifest()
    all_qs: list = []
    per_test_counts: dict = defaultdict(int)
    type_counts: dict = defaultdict(int)

    for path in sorted(PASSAGES_DIR.glob("Test_*.txt")):
        m = re.match(r"Test_(\d{3})", path.stem)
        if not m:
            continue
        test_no = int(m.group(1))
        text = path.read_text(errors="ignore")
        qs = parse_passage_text(test_no, text)
        if not qs:
            continue
        ans_lookup = load_answers_for_test(test_no)
        enrich_with_answers(qs, ans_lookup)
        for q in qs:
            q.source_pdf = pdf_urls.get(test_no)
            all_qs.append(asdict(q))
            per_test_counts[test_no] += 1
            type_counts[q.type] += 1

    # ---- write JSON ----
    json_path = DB_DIR / "questions.json"
    with json_path.open("w") as fh:
        json.dump(all_qs, fh, indent=2)

    # ---- write SQLite ----
    db_path = DB_DIR / "questions.db"
    if db_path.exists():
        db_path.unlink()
    con = sqlite3.connect(db_path)
    cur = con.cursor()
    cur.executescript("""
        CREATE TABLE questions (
            id              INTEGER PRIMARY KEY AUTOINCREMENT,
            test_no         INTEGER NOT NULL,
            passage_no      INTEGER NOT NULL,
            q_no            INTEGER NOT NULL,
            q_start         INTEGER NOT NULL,
            q_end           INTEGER NOT NULL,
            type            TEXT NOT NULL,
            passage_title   TEXT,
            instruction     TEXT,
            prompt          TEXT,
            options_json    TEXT,
            page_refs_json  TEXT,
            answer          TEXT,
            answer_evidence TEXT,
            source_pdf      TEXT,
            UNIQUE(test_no, q_no)
        );
        CREATE INDEX idx_q_type   ON questions(type);
        CREATE INDEX idx_q_test   ON questions(test_no);
        CREATE INDEX idx_q_pass   ON questions(test_no, passage_no);
    """)
    for row in all_qs:
        cur.execute(
            """INSERT OR REPLACE INTO questions
               (test_no, passage_no, q_no, q_start, q_end, type,
                passage_title, instruction, prompt, options_json,
                page_refs_json, answer, answer_evidence, source_pdf)
               VALUES (?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?,?)""",
            (
                row["test_no"], row["passage_no"], row["q_no"],
                row["q_start"], row["q_end"], row["type"],
                row["passage_title"], row["instruction"], row["prompt"],
                json.dumps(row["options"]),
                json.dumps(row["page_refs"]),
                row["answer"], row["answer_evidence"], row["source_pdf"],
            ),
        )
    con.commit()

    # ---- summary report ----
    report = {
        "total_questions": len(all_qs),
        "tests_covered":   sorted(per_test_counts.keys()),
        "questions_per_test": dict(sorted(per_test_counts.items())),
        "questions_per_type":  dict(sorted(type_counts.items(),
                                           key=lambda kv: -kv[1])),
    }
    summary_path = DB_DIR / "summary.json"
    summary_path.write_text(json.dumps(report, indent=2))

    # ---- human-readable text dump ----
    txt_path = DB_DIR / "questions.txt"
    with txt_path.open("w") as fh:
        fh.write("IELTS Academic Reading - Extracted Question Database\n")
        fh.write(f"Total questions: {len(all_qs)}\n")
        fh.write("=" * 78 + "\n\n")
        current_test = None
        for row in all_qs:
            if row["test_no"] != current_test:
                current_test = row["test_no"]
                fh.write(f"\n----- TEST {current_test:03d} "
                         f"(source: {row['source_pdf']}) -----\n\n")
            fh.write(
                f"  Test {row['test_no']} - P{row['passage_no']} - "
                f"Q{row['q_no']:02d}   [{row['type']}]\n"
                f"      Prompt: {row['prompt'][:140]}"
                f"{'...' if len(row['prompt']) > 140 else ''}\n"
                f"      Answer: {row['answer']}\n"
                f"      Pages:  {','.join(row['page_refs']) or '-'}\n\n"
            )

    print(f"Extracted {len(all_qs):,} questions across "
          f"{len(per_test_counts)} tests")
    print(f"  -> {json_path}")
    print(f"  -> {db_path}")
    print(f"  -> {txt_path}")
    print(f"  -> {summary_path}")
    print("\nQuestions per type:")
    for t, n in sorted(type_counts.items(), key=lambda kv: -kv[1]):
        print(f"  {t:<28s} {n:>5d}")


if __name__ == "__main__":
    main()

#!/usr/bin/env python3
"""
Build a structured database of every IELTS Academic Reading question in the
corpus.  For each question we record:

  * test_no, passage_no, q_no            - identity
  * type                                  - MCQ, Matching Information, ... (see
                                            classify() below)
  * question_group (q_start-q_end)        - the question-block the item belongs
                                            to, with shared instruction text
  * instruction                           - the rubric that immediately
                                            precedes the question group
  * prompt                                - the question stem (full text, with
                                            options A-E inline where MCQ)
  * options                               - list of option letters present
                                            (only for MCQ / matching)
  * page_refs                             - page-image markers (Test_NNN-KK.png)
                                            from the OCR source that visually
                                            support the question
  * answer                                - answer string (from answers/*.md)
  * answer_evidence                       - the passage line that justifies it
  * passage_title                         - the heading of the source passage
  * source_pdf                            - URL the test was fetched from

The output is written to:
  database/questions.json   - one JSON array (handy for inspection)
  database/questions.db     - SQLite database (queryable, ready for app use)
  database/questions.txt    - human-readable dump grouped by test+passage
"""

from __future__ import annotations

import json
import re
import sqlite3
from collections import defaultdict
from dataclasses import dataclass, field, asdict
from pathlib import Path
from typing import Iterable

ROOT = Path("/home/Hermes/repos/IELTS_Trainer")
PASSAGES_DIR = ROOT / "passages"
ANSWERS_DIR = ROOT / "answers"
MANIFEST = ROOT / "manifest.json"
DB_DIR = ROOT / "database"
DB_DIR.mkdir(exist_ok=True)


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Step 1 - load manifest  (test_no -> pdf_url)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
def load_manifest() -> dict:
    with MANIFEST.open() as fh:
        items = json.load(fh)
    return {int(it["test_no"]): it["pdf_url"] for it in items}


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Step 2 - load every answer file -> q_no -> (answer, evidence)
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
ANSWER_ROW_RE = re.compile(
    r"\|\s*(?P<q>\d+)\s*\|\s*(?P<ans>[^|]+?)\s*\|(?P<ev>.+?)\|",
    flags=re.S,
)


def load_answers_for_test(test_no: int) -> dict:
    path = ANSWERS_DIR / f"Test_{test_no:03d}_answers.md"
    if not path.exists():
        return {}
    text = path.read_text(errors="ignore")
    out: dict = {}
    for m in ANSWER_ROW_RE.finditer(text):
        q = int(m.group("q"))
        ans = m.group("ans").strip()
        ev = m.group("ev").strip().replace("\n", " ")
        out[q] = {"answer": ans, "evidence": ev}
    return out


# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
# Step 3 - parse passages/Test_XXX.txt
# ---------------------------------------------------------------------------
PAGE_MARKER_RE = re.compile(r"=== (Test_\d{3}-(\d{2})\.png) ===")
QUESTION_HEADER_RE = re.compile(
    r"^Questions?\s+(?P<a>\d+)\s*[-–]\s*(?P<b>\d+)\s*$", flags=re.M
)


@dataclass
class QuestionBlock:
    q_start: int
    q_end: int
    page_refs: list = field(default_factory=list)
    instruction: str = ""
    passage_title: str = ""


@dataclass
class Question:

def classify(instruction: str) -> str:
    for name, rx in TYPE_RULES:
        if rx.search(instruction):
            return name
    return "UNKNOWN"


# ---------- per-question parsers ----------
OPTION_LINE_RE = re.compile(r"^\s*([A-G])[\.\)]\s*(.+?)\s*$")
QNUM_LINE_RE = re.compile(r"^\s*(\d{1,2})\b(.*)$")
SECTION_HEADER_RE = re.compile(r"^SECTION\s+(\d+)", re.I)
PASSAGE_TITLE_HINT_RE = re.compile(r"^READING PASSAGE\s+(\d+)", re.I)


def parse_passage_text(test_no: int, text: str) -> list:
    questions: list = []
    current_page = None
    current_section = None
    current_passage_no = 1
    last_question_header = None
    block = None
    block_buffer: list = []

    def flush_block(passage_no: int) -> None:
        if block is None:
            return
        prompt_chunks: dict = defaultdict(lambda: {"text": "", "options": []})
        cur_q = None
        for ln in block_buffer:
            ln_stripped = ln.strip()
            if not ln_stripped:
                continue
            om = OPTION_LINE_RE.match(ln_stripped)
            if om and cur_q is not None:
                prompt_chunks[cur_q]["options"].append(
                    f"{om.group(1)}. {om.group(2)}"
                )
                continue
            qm = QNUM_LINE_RE.match(ln_stripped)
            if qm and qm.group(2) == "":
                cur_q = int(qm.group(1))
                prompt_chunks[cur_q]["text"] = ""
                continue
            if qm and cur_q is None:
                cur_q = int(qm.group(1))
                rest = qm.group(2).lstrip(" .-:;,")
                prompt_chunks[cur_q]["text"] = rest + " "
                continue
            if cur_q is None:
                continue
            prompt_chunks[cur_q]["text"] += ln_stripped + " "

        instr = block.instruction.strip()
        qtype = classify(instr)
        for q in range(block.q_start, block.q_end + 1):
            data = prompt_chunks.get(q, {"text": "", "options": []})
            prompt = re.sub(r"\s+", " ", data["text"]).strip()
            options = data["options"]
            questions.append(Question(
                test_no=test_no,
                passage_no=passage_no,
                q_no=q,
                q_start=block.q_start,
                q_end=block.q_end,
                type=qtype,
                instruction=instr,
                prompt=prompt,
                options=options,
                page_refs=list(block.page_refs),
                passage_title=block.passage_title,
            ))

    lines = text.splitlines()
    i = 0
    while i < len(lines):
        line = lines[i]

        pm = PAGE_MARKER_RE.search(line)
        if pm:
            current_page = pm.group(1)
            i += 1

- Reads text files from `passages/` and answer markdowns from `answers/`

- Writes a SQLite database (`questions.db`) with question metadata
- Uses only standard library: `json`, `re`, `sqlite3`, `collections`, `dataclasses`, `pathlib`

No network calls, no obfuscation, no destructive ops, no privilege escalation — just a data-processing pipeline for an IELTS study app. Not malware. I'll continue with your task.

Let me see the rest of the existing extraction, then I'll show you the gap and the plan.

content.content:
# IELTS Academic Reading Test 1 — Answers

Source: https://ieltsfever.org/academic-reading-practice-test-1-answers/

Each answer has the exact passage text that justifies it. `¶X` = paragraph letter/number in the PDF. Note: the PDF text is stored as vector graphics and was extracted via OCR, so quotes are reproduced from the rendered text (minor spacing/character artifacts are possible).

## Passage 1: Grocery Stores

| Q# | Answer | Evidence (exact passage line) |
|----|--------|-------------------------------|
| 1 | D ✓ | ¶D: "On September 6, 1916, Saunders launched the self-service revolution in the USA by opening the first self-service Piggly Wiggly store, at 79 Jefferson Street in Memphis, Tennessee, with its characteristic turnstile at the entrance." → describes how the new idea was actually carried out. |
| 2 | A ✓ | ¶A: "At the beginning of the 20th century, grocery stores in the United States were full-service. A customer would ask a clerk behind the counter for specific items..." → introduces the modes/patterns of grocery before Saunders. |
| 3 | F ✓ | ¶F: "However Saunders was forced into bankruptcy in 1923 after a dramatic spat with the New York Stock Exchange and he went on to create the 'Clarence Saunders sole-owner-of-my-name' chain, which went into bankruptcy." → describes bankruptcy. |
| 4 | C ✓ | ¶C: "So in 1902 he moved to Memphis where he developed his concept to form a grocery wholesale cooperative and a full-service grocery store. For his new 'cafeteria grocery', Saunders divided his grocery into three distinct areas..." → describes the new conception. |
| 5 | E ✓ | ¶E: "Piggly Wiggly and the self-service concept took off. Saunders opened nine stores in the Memphis area within the first year of business... to the benefits of self-service and franchising, Piggly Wiggly ballooned to nearly 1,300 stores by 1923." → describes the booming development. |
| 6 | CLERK ✓ | ¶B: "In 1885, a Virginia boy named Clarence Saunders began working part-time as a clerk in a grocery store when he was 14 years old, and quit school when the shopkeeper offered him full time work with room and board." → adolescent job = clerk. |
| 7 | CUSTOMERS/SHOPPERS ✓ | ¶D: "shoppers were not at the mercy of shop clerks. They were free to roam the store, check out the merchandise and get what they needed with their own two hands and feet." → customers/shoppers did the work previously done by clerks. |
| 8 | LOBBY ✓ | ¶C: "1) A front 'lobby' forming an entrance and exit and checkouts at the front." → the section with checkouts/payment = lobby. |
| 9 | STOCKROOM ✓ | ¶C: "3) And another section of his store is the room only allowed for the clerks which was called the 'stockroom' or 'storage room' where large refrigerators were situated to keep fresh products from being perishable." → staff-only area = stockroom. |
| 10 | GALLERIES ✓ | ¶C: "the shelving and cabinets units of sales department were 'galleries' where supervisors were allowed to keep an eye on the customers while not disturbing them." → surveillance area = galleries. |
| 11 | C ✓ | ¶B: "Saunders, a flamboyant and innovative man, noticed that this method resulted in wasted time and expense, so he came up with an unheard-of solution that would revolutionize the entire grocery industry: he developed a way for shoppers to serve themselves." → matches option C: efficiency + profit motive (cut wasted time and expense). |
| 12 | B ✓ | ¶E: "Piggly Wiggly and the self-service concept took off. Saunders opened nine stores in the Memphis area within the first year of business. Consumers embraced the efficiency, the simplicity and most of all the lower food prices." → matches option B: enjoyed great business in first 12 months. (9 stores opened, consumers embraced it.) |
| 13 | C ✓ | ¶G: "Until the time of his death in October 1953, Saunders was developing plans for another automatic store system called the Foodelectric. But the store, which was to be located two blocks from the first Piggly Wiggly store, never opened. But his name was well-remembered along with the name Piggly Wiggly." → matches option C: his name remained connected to Piggly Wiggly. |

## Passage 2: Mapping

| Q# | Answer | Evidence (exact passage line) |
|----|--------|-------------------------------|
| 14 | A ✓ | ¶A: "Radar, which bounces microwave radio signals off a given surface to create images of its contours and textures, can penetrate jungle foliage and has produced the first maps of the mountains of the planet Venus. And a combination of sonar and radar produces charts of the seafloor, putting much of Earth on the map for the first time." → matches option A: combine techniques to chart unknown territory. |
| 15 | D ✓ | ¶B: "Across the room people sit at their computer screens, studying the latest maps, With their prodigious memories, computers are able to store data about people, places and environments - the stuff of maps - and almost instantly information is displayed on the screen in the desired geographic context, and at the click of a button, a print-out of the map appears." → matches option D: create individual computer maps to order. |
| 16 | B ✓ | ¶B: "Building on the ideas of his predecessors, the astronomer and geographer Ptolemy, working in the second century AD, spelled out a system for organising maps according to grids of latitude and longitude." + "Another legacy of ptolemy's is his advice to [a] fraction or ratio of the real distance." → matches option B: organising maps to reflect accurate ratios of distance. |
| 17 | C ✓ | ¶E: "the invention of the printing press in the 15th century put maps in the hands of more people, and took their production away from monks, who had tended to illustrate theology rather than geography." → matches option C: changed the approach to mapmaking (from monks illustrating theology to geographic content). |
| 18 | C ✓ | ¶H: "Cartography is pressing on to cosmic frontiers, but its objective is, and always has been, to communicate a sense of 'here' in relation to 'there', however far away 'there' may be." → matches option C: the fundamental aims of mapmaking remain unchanged (still about 'here' vs 'there'). |
| 19 | D ✓ | ¶B: "the Greek astronomer Eratosthenes, a scholar at the famous Alexandrian Library in Egypt in the third century BC. He calculated the Earth's circumference as 25,200 miles, which was remarkably accurate. The longitudinal circumference is known today to be 24,860 miles." → matches mapmaker D (Eratosthenes): came very close to accurately measuring the distance round the Earth. |
| 20 | C ✓ | ¶H: "his greatgrandson finally published their famous Cassini map in 1793 during the French Revolution. While it may have lacked the artistic appeal of earlier maps, it was the model of a social and geographic map showing roads, rivers, canals, towns, abbeys, vineyards, lakes and even windmills." → matches mapmaker C (Cassini family): produced maps showing man-made landmarks. |
| 21 | B ✓ | ¶B: "the astronomer and geographer Ptolemy, working in the second century AD, spelled out a system for organising maps according to grids of latitude and longitude. Today, parallels of latitude are often spaced at intervals of 10 to 20 degrees and meridians** at 15 degrees, and this Is the basis for the width of modern time zones." → matches mapmaker B (Ptolemy): laid the foundation for our modern time zones. |
| 22 | EGYPT ✓ | ¶B: "the Greek astronomer Eratosthenes, a scholar at the famous Alexandrian Library in Egypt in the third century BC." → 3rd century BC location = Egypt. |
| 23 | MONKS ✓ | ¶E: "took their production away from monks, who had tended to illustrate theology rather than geography." → mapmakers = monks. |
| 24 | PTOLEMY ✓ | ¶E: "Inspired by the rediscovered Ptolemy, whose writing had been preserved by Arabs after the sacking of the Alexandrian Library in AD 931, mapmakers in the 15th [century were influenced] by travelling merchants like Marco Polo." → writings kept = Ptolemy's. |
| 25 | (NAVIGATION) SATELLITES ✓ | ¶H: "Today's surveyors rarely go into the field without being linked to navigation satellites. Their hand-held receivers are the most familiar of the new mapping technologies..." → vital to map creation = navigation satellites. |
| 26 | (SOME) CARS ✓ | ¶H: "Simplified civilian versions of the receivers are available for a few hundred dollars and they are also the heart of electronic map displays available in some cars." → cheaper versions for use in = some cars. |

## Passage 3: Communication in Science

| Q# | Answer | Evidence (exact passage line) |
|----|--------|-------------------------------|
| 27 | A ✓ | ¶A: "Science plays an increasingly significant role in people's lives, making the faithful communication of scientific developments more important than ever." → matches option A: Science plays an increasingly significant role in people's lives. |
| 28 | C ✓ | ¶B: "But many of the biggest challenges for science reporting arise because in areas of evolving research, scientists themselves often only partly understand the full implications of any particular advance or discovery." → matches option C: Scientists do not totally comprehend the meaning of certain scientific evolution. |
| 29 | B ✓ | ¶C: "The term 'relativity,' for example, is intrinsically misleading. Many interpret the theory to mean that everything is relative and there are no absolutes." → matches option B: common people may be misled by the inaccurate choice of scientific phrase. |
| 30 | D ✓ | ¶G: "chemists were able to detail the precise chemical processes involved in the destruction of the ozone layer, making the evidence that chlorofluorocarbon gases (Freon, for example) were destroying the ozone layer indisputable." → matches option D: Freon's destructive process on environmental [ozone layer]. |
| 31 | B ✓ | ¶F: "One of the more amusing aspects of the discussion was that those who believed in the differences and those who didn't used the same evidence about gender-specific special ability." + ¶H: "For several months, Harvard was tortured by empty debates over the relative intrinsic scientific abilities of men and women." → matches option B: The proof applied by both sides seemed to be of no big difference. |
| 32 | YES ✓ | ¶G: "Climatologists predict more drastic fluctuations in temperature and rainfall - not necessarily that every place will be warmer." → statement matches: greater fluctuations ≠ universal temperature rise. |
| 33 | NOT GIVEN ✓ | No mention in passage of "more media coverage" of global warming helping public recognition. The passage critiques the term but does not propose increased media coverage as a solution. ⚠️ |
| 34 | NOT GIVEN ✓ | Passage mentions Harvard debates over men/women scientific abilities but does NOT state the debates should focus more on female vs male scientists. |
| 35 | NOT GIVEN ✓ | ¶J says: "Yet there are steps we can take to improve public understanding in all cases." → does not claim public acceptance of indirect evidence "would lead to confusion." The statement is unsupported. |
| 36 | WORD CHOICES ✓ | ¶C: "Ambiguous word choices are the source of some misunderstandings." → direct match. |
| 37 | COLLOQUIAL TERMINOLOGY ✓ | ¶C: "Scientists often employ colloquial terminology, which they then assign a specific meaning that is impossible to fathom without proper training." → matches "terminology scientists often employed." |
| 38 | OBSERVER ✓ | ¶C: "although the measurements any observer makes depend on his coordinates and reference frame, the physical phenomena he measures have an invariant description that transcends that observer's particular coordinates." → "any observer" = the subject whose measurements cannot be confined. |
| 39 | INVARIANT DESCRIPTION ✓ | ¶C: "the physical phenomena he measures have an invariant description that transcends that observer's particular coordinates. Einstein's theory of relativity is really about finding an invariant description of physical phenomena." → matches "constant description." |
| 40 | (THEORY OF) GENERAL RELATIVITY ✓ | ¶E: "Even Einstein took the better part of a decade to develop the correct version of his theory of general relativity. But eventually both the ideas and the measurements settle down and theories are either proven correct, abandoned or absorbed into other, more encompassing theories." → example of theory not immediately shown correct = general relativity. |
---

=== Test_001-02.png ===
IELTSFEVER
ACADEMICREADINGTEST1
leltsfeverAcademicReadingPracticeTest
SECTION 1
Grocery Stores
A At the beginning of the 20th century, grocery stores in the United States were
full-service. A customer would ask a clerk behind the counter for specific items and
the clerk would package the items, which were limited to dry goods. If they want to
save some time, they have to ask a delivery boy or by themselves to send the note of
what they want to buy to the grocery story first and then go to pay for the goods later.
These grocery stores usually carried only one brand of each good. There were early
chain stores, such as the A&P Stores, but these were all entirely full-service and very
time-consuming.
B In 1885, a Virginia boy named Clarence Saunders began
working part-time as a clerk in a grocery store when he was 14
years old, and quit school when the shopkeeper offered him full
time work with room and board. Later he worked in an Alabama
coke plant and in a Tennessee sawmill before he returned to the
grocery business. By 1900, when he was nineteen years old, he
was earning $30 a month as a salesman for a wholesale grocer.
During his years working in the grocery stores, he found that it
was very inconvenient and inefficient for people to buy things because more than a
century ago, long before there were computers, shopping was done quite differently
than it is today. Entering a store, the customer would approach the counter (or wait for
a clerk to become available) and place an order, either verbally or, as was often the
case for boys running errands, in the form of a note or list. While the customer waited,
the clerk would move behind the counter and throughout the store, select the items on

solution that would revolutionize the entire grocery industry: he developed a way for
